result.meeting_context: City Council Special meeting to interview Environmental Planning Commission (EPC) applicant candidates. Seven applicants were interviewed using a panel format with standardized questions, followed by council deliberation and voting to recommend two candidates for four-year terms.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: City is piloting Zoom automated captioning for this meeting with disclaimer about accuracy
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : To interview Environmental Planning Commission applicant applicants, the City is piloting the use of Zoom automated captioning for this meeting.

points.speaker: Heather Glaser
points.text: Council has quorum with Council Member Schwalter absent
points.source: Heather Glaser : You have a quorum with Council Member Schwalter absent.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Council will interview 7 applicants using panel format with same time allocation and randomized round Robin order
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : The Council will interview 7 applicants for the Environmental Planning Commission for the selection process outlined in Council Policy Number K2.

points.speaker: Lisa Matichak
points.text: Matichak suggested modifying first question to ask incumbents what they've contributed versus asking non-incumbents what they would bring
points.source: Lisa Matichak : So maybe it could be amended to include what have you personally done to make a contribution to the EPC or if you are not an incumbent, what what do you think you would bring to the EPC

points.speaker: Heather Glaser
points.text: Policy requires asking everyone same questions with same time allocation for fairness
points.source: Heather Glaser : Yeah, the policy discussed is asking everyone the same questions and giving everyone the same amount of time.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: First question modified to ask what candidates have contributed or would bring that is unique to EPC
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : So the first question would be modified to what have you personally done to make a contribution to the EPC or if you are not currently serving, what would you bring to the EPC that is unique

points.speaker: Anne Cavanaugh
points.text: Cavanaugh brings 22 years Mountain View residency, downtown resident and small landlord perspective, and talent for perspective taking
points.source: Anne Cavanaugh : So I think probably the most unique thing that I would bring to the EPCI mean I've been a resident of Mountain View for many years, 22 years.

points.speaker: Bill Cranston
points.text: Cranston brings small business owner perspective with employees in below market rate and moderate income categories
points.source: Bill Cranston : I'm a small business owner and one of the things that has what I've tried to do is bring the perspective of somebody who's trying to work with with that has employees who are trying to live in the Bay Area.

points.speaker: Paul Donahue
points.text: Donahue has 16 years experience on city advisory bodies including 7 years PRC, 8 years library board, and 9 years Airport Land Use Commission
points.source: Paul Donahue : I've been involved in the city on various advisory bodies for the last 16 years, seven years on the PRC, eight years on the library board.

points.speaker: Jose Gutierrez
points.text: Gutierrez is single parent, 22+ year community member, ethnic minority, and has worked on affordable housing partnerships including charities program
points.source: Jose Gutierrez : I'm a single parent. I'm got two beautiful girls. I've been a member of the community for now over 22 plus years.

points.speaker: Silja Paymer
points.text: Paymer brings environmental science teaching background, daily bike commuting experience, and inside view of city-school relationships
points.source: Silja Paymer : And I think one unique perspective I bring is that I've been an environmental science teacher.

points.speaker: Molly Smith
points.text: Smith is newer resident from Boulder with environmental studies degree and train commuting experience
points.source: Molly Smith : I am newer to the area. I have been here just over 2 years. I've came from Boulder, Co

points.speaker: Li Zhang
points.text: Zhang has lived in Mountain View over 20 years, has PhD in chemistry, and wants to bring sustainability perspective and remove housing development restraints
points.source: Li Zhang : I also lived in Mountain View for over 20 years, and I've lived here since I was single.

topics.topic: General Plan and Precise Plans Discussion

points.speaker: Bill Cranston
points.text: General Plan implemented 8 years ago with change areas including Bayshore, San Antonio, El Camino and E Wiseman having significant development impact
points.source: Bill Cranston : So the the general plan was put in place about eight years ago. A lot of things were included in that with the the change areas

points.speaker: Jose Gutierrez
points.text: Gutierrez supports General Plan revision due to changed times and COVID impacts, believes it would strengthen city's negotiating position with developers
points.source: Jose Gutierrez : I like to see it revised. I like for us to be able to, for the City Council to sit down together as a team, work through that because times have changed, needs have changed.

points.speaker: Paul Donahue
points.text: Donahue opposes reopening General Plan, noting it took 4 years to complete and shouldn't spend 4 out of every 10 years redoing it
points.source: Paul Donahue : I mean the the General Plan was put in place about 10 years ago and I my recollection is it took about four years to to get that thing through

points.speaker: Li Zhang
points.text: Zhang notes General Plan finalized in 2012, reworking would take 3-4 years, and council needs to evaluate available resources
points.source: Li Zhang : I understood the general plan was this, I was finalized in 2012, it was quite some time ago, but it was a visionary plan.

topics.topic: Property Rights and Community Benefits Balance

points.speaker: Paul Donahue
points.text: Donahue generally leans toward public open space over private as it benefits entire community
points.source: Paul Donahue : generally I would, I would lean towards public open space that can be used by all of the the community

points.speaker: Silja Paymer
points.text: Paymer leans toward public benefit in denser development since residents need shared spaces when lacking private backyards
points.source: Silja Paymer : So I think probably if it was a set of scales, I'd probably learn more towards public benefit when I was thinking about the property rights, especially as we're going more dense.

points.speaker: Bill Cranston
points.text: Cranston believes property owners should work within existing rules, but density bonus law limits cities' ability to ask for community benefits
points.source: Bill Cranston : So when it comes to public versus private, I'm a pretty strong believer in that somebody who owns property should be able to look at the rules that the city has

points.speaker: Ellen Kamei
points.text: Kamei recommends Commission training 101 and clarity on EPC's advisory capacity questions
points.source: Ellen Kamei : So I I did take the opportunity to watch several EPC meetings and thought that something I believe the the city clerk has brought up and I believe our city manager has brought up or worth pursuing and I'm not sure how we do that which is a a Commission or board training 101.

points.speaker: Alison Hicks
points.text: Hicks praised Cranston for asking smartest questions during Google walking tour and EPC meetings
points.source: Alison Hicks : I have to say I first became familiar with his work when I was on a Google walking tour with a bunch of people and he was just asking, I have to say this, the smartest questions in the tour group.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Ramirez noted Molly and Ann have been recommended for appointments to other advisory bodies
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : It didn't make a difference in this context, but I think it is important to note that both, if unless I'm mistaken, correct me if I'm wrong, Council Appointments Review committee. But Molly and Ann have been recommended for appointments to other advisory bodies

topics.topic: Final Voting Results and Next Steps

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Council voted to recommend Bill Cranston and Jose Gutierrez for reappointment to four-year EPC terms
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : So congratulations, Commissioner Cranston, Unless the Council deviates from this recommendation, it looks like you will have an opportunity to continue your service.

points.speaker: Heather Glaser
points.text: Formal resolution for reappointments will be brought to Council on December 13th
points.source: Heather Glaser : So on December 13, we'll bring a resolution to the council formally reappointing Bill and Jose for those next terms.

points.speaker: Lucas Ramirez
points.text: Next City Council meeting scheduled for December 6th, 2022, meeting adjourned at 6:38 PM
points.source: Lucas Ramirez : The next City Council meeting will be held on December 6th, 2022. Until then, stay healthy. The meeting is adjourned at 6:38 PM.

decisions.text: Council will bring formal resolution on December 13th to reappoint Bill Cranston and Jose Gutierrez for four-year EPC terms
decisions.source: Heather Glaser : So on December 13, we'll bring a resolution to the council formally reappointing Bill and Jose for those next terms.
